desc: Common drone fly — the most successful hover fly on Earth. Such a convincing honeybee mimic that beekeepers frequently mistake it. Found on every continent except Antarctica. Larvae are the classic 'rat-tailed maggot' in standing water. Adults excellent pollinators.

fm.Mimicry: Honeybee mimic; amber-brown, variable dark marks

hp: Associated with Apis mellifera (honeybees) — commensal or parasite.

desc: Transverse drone fly — large drone fly with dark transverse facial stripe distinguishing it from similar Eristalis species. On late-season composites. Effective bee mimic.

fm.Mimicry: Bee mimic; dark face stripe vs. E. tenax

hp: Honey bee mimic — Batesian mimicry. Adult pollinates flowers. Rat-tailed maggot larva in stagnant water.

desc: Narcissus bulb fly — convincing bumblebee mimic with dense fuzzy body. Multiple color morphs mimic different Bombus species. Larvae bore into and destroy daffodil, narcissus, and amaryllis bulbs. Major pest of ornamental bulbs. Introduced from Europe.

fm.Shape: Round, densely hairy; ½ in–⅔ in

hp: Bumblebee mimic — Batesian mimicry. Larvae bore into narcissus and lily bulbs. Horticultural pest.

desc: Yellowjacket hover fly — North America's largest hover fly, a spectacular yellowjacket mimic. Completely harmless despite alarming appearance. On autumn flowers.

fm.Shape: Large, wasp-like; ⅔ in–1 in (largest NA syrphid)

hp: Ambushes prey on flowers. Largest.

desc: Maize calligrapher — the most abundant hover fly in North America. Tiny — easily overlooked despite ubiquity. Bright yellow and black. Adults pollinate many crops. Larvae eat aphids. Found in virtually every LA garden, park, and agricultural field.

fm.Mimicry: Yellow-green with thin black abdominal bands
fm.Shape: Small, slender; ⅛ in–¼ in; precise hover

hp: Aphid predator — important biological control agent in crops and gardens. Preys on aphid. Tiny.

desc: Oblique-banded hover fly — sleek syrphid with distinctive oblique yellow stripes on abdomen (diagnostic field mark). Larvae eat aphids. One of the most recognizable hover flies.

fm.Mimicry: Black with oblique yellow stripes; yellow thorax

hp: Builds horizontal sheet/dome web. Preys on aphids. Family Syrphinae.

desc: American hover fly — medium syrphid with bright yellow lunule-shaped marks on dark abdomen. One of the most important aphid biocontrol agents in California agriculture — larvae consume hundreds of aphids per development cycle. Adults pollinate crops.

fm.Mimicry: Black with paired yellow crescent lunules

hp: American hover fly — larvae consume hundreds of aphids per individual. One of the most important natural aphid biocontrol agents in CA agriculture.

desc: Opinionated hover fly — bright yellow-banded syrphid with the classic 'hoverfly' appearance. Hovers motionlessly at flowers. Larvae voraciously consume aphids. Very common.

fm.Mimicry: Broad yellow bands on black; wasp-like

hp: Ambushes prey on flowers. Preys on aphids. Family Syrphinae.

desc: Common thick-legged hover fly — tiny syrphid with remarkably swollen hind femora (diagnostic). One of the most common and overlooked hover flies. At flowers everywhere.

fm.Shape: Small; ¼ in–⅓ in. Hind femora noticeably swollen.

hp: Flower pollinator and hover fly mimic. Larvae variable. Most Common.

desc: Large, dark, bee-mimicking hoverfly. Larvae develop in rotting cactus pads — important ecological role in desert edge.

fm.Mimicry: Large dark bee mimic; iridescent sheen

hp: Specialist on cactus (cactus spp.). On Opuntia (cactus). Larvae develop in rotting cactus pads — important.

desc: Spotted-wing hover fly — large handsome hoverfly with distinctive white comma-shaped marks on black abdomen. A long-distance migratory species — populations irrupt northward from breeding grounds, sometimes in spectacular mass flights. Larvae eat aphids.

fm.Mimicry: Dark abdomen with white comma-shaped lunules (not yellow)
fm.Wing: Spurious vein; clear; sometimes darkened stigma

hp: Builds horizontal sheet/dome web. Preys on aphids.

desc: Hornet hover fly — remarkable bumblebee mimic that infiltrates bumblebee nests to lay eggs. Larvae scavenge dead bees, pollen, and nest debris. Multiple color morphs each mimic a different bumblebee species — an example of Müllerian/Batesian mimicry.

fm.Mimicry: Convincing bumblebee mimic; hairy; yellow/orange and black bands

hp: Bumblebee mimic that infiltrates Bombus nests. Larvae scavenge nest debris. Brood parasite.

desc: Western calligrapher hover fly — small hover fly with intricate black calligraphy-like markings on yellow abdomen. Hovers at flowers. Larvae eat aphids. Common in gardens.
hp: Builds horizontal sheet/dome web. Ambushes prey on flowers. In gardens. Preys on aphids.

desc: Striped sun fly — medium hover fly with bold yellow thoracic stripes. Rat-tailed maggot larvae in stagnant water. Visits flowers. Bee-mimic.
hp: Ambushes prey on flowers. Ant mimic — may prey on ants. Bee mimic.

desc: European drone fly — medium hover fly with orange lateral abdominal marks. Introduced from Europe. Excellent bee mimic. Larvae ('rat-tailed maggots') in stagnant water.

fm.Color: Dark with orange lateral marks; bee-mimic

hp: Ant mimic — may prey on ants. Bee mimic.

desc: Long-horned hover fly — tiny, very dark syrphid with elongated abdomen. Among the smallest and most overlooked hover flies despite being extremely common. Larvae are effective aphid predators. Adults visit small flowers. Important biological control.

fm.Mimicry: Small, dark, elongated; reddish abdomen tip
fm.Shape: ⅛ in–¼ in; very small; elongated

hp: Biocontrol agent; aphid predator. Preys on aphid. Elongated.

desc: Spotted-eye hover fly — immediately recognizable by spectacularly spotted/banded compound eyes (diagnostic — no other LA hover fly has this). Medium-sized drone fly. On flowers. Larvae aquatic (rat-tailed maggot type). Introduced, now common.
hp: Freshwater aquatic habitat — streams and ponds.

desc: Long-nosed hover fly — unmistakable syrphid with elongated snout-like face projection housing a long proboscis. Orange abdomen. Visits tubular flowers that most hoverflies cannot access. Larvae in cow dung. Introduced from Europe.

fm.Role: Pollinator of tubular flowers; long snout reaches nectar

hp: On dung — nutrient recycler and parasite control.

desc: Narrow-banded hover fly — large syrphid with yellow abdominal bands narrower and more clearly separated than Syrphus species. Larvae are voracious aphid predators — a single larva can consume hundreds of aphids. Adults excellent pollinators on umbellifers and composites.

fm.Mimicry: Yellow bands on black; narrower than Syrphus

hp: Narrow-banded hover fly — adult pollinator visiting many flowers. Larvae are voracious aphid predators. Important dual biocontrol/pollination.

desc: Dark-footed hover fly — small, dark syrphid. Males have distinctive flattened front tarsi used in courtship. Larvae prey on aphids in leaf litter at plant bases. Very common in gardens but overlooked due to small size. Important biological control agent.
hp: Dark-footed hover fly — aphid predator as larva, flower pollinator as adult. Males have distinctive flattened front tarsi for courtship.

desc: Chrysotoxum hover fly — striking wasp-mimic syrphid with yellow bands and unusually long, forward-pointing antennae (unique among hover flies). Larvae feed on root aphids underground. Adults visit flowers. The long antennae enhance the wasp mimicry.

fm.Description: Striking wasp-mimic hoverfly with yellow bands

hp: Ant mimic — Batesian mimicry in body shape and gait.

desc: Globe-tailed hover fly — small slender syrphid named for the globe-shaped male genitalia. Bright yellow and black. Larvae voracious aphid predators in gardens and agricultural fields. Adults important flower visitors. Very common but small.

fm.Description: Small slender hoverfly with globe-shaped male genitalia

hp: Aphid predator — important biocontrol agent. Beneficial in gardens. Preys on aphid. Family Syrphinae.

desc: Four-spotted pipiza — small dark hover fly. Larvae feed on woolly aphids hidden in bark crevices. Adults at flowers. Less conspicuous than yellow-banded species.
hp: Ambushes prey on flowers. On tree bark. Preys on aphids.

desc: Cloudy-winged hover fly — small dark syrphid with pale yellow lunar spots on abdomen. Larvae are voracious aphid predators — among the most important biological control agents in California. Very common garden visitor. Adults pollinate many plants.
hp: Biocontrol agent; pollinator; aphid predator

desc: Dark drone fly — a convincing honeybee mimic, darker than the Common Drone Fly. Larvae are aquatic 'rat-tailed maggots' with telescoping breathing tubes in stagnant water. Adults visit flowers. Common but less well-known than E. tenax.

fm.Color: Dark with pale markings; bee mimic

hp: Freshwater aquatic habitat — streams and ponds. Preys on ant.

desc: Small hover fly with convergent yellow abdominal bands. Larvae are voracious aphid predators. One of the most common garden hover flies in SoCal.

fm.Color: Yellow-black; converging yellow bands on abdomen
fm.Habitat: Gardens, agricultural fields — wherever aphids occur

hp: Aphid predator — important biocontrol agent. Beneficial in gardens. Preys on aphid. Family Syrphinae.

desc: Tiny introduced hover fly from Palearctic. Very common in gardens. Larvae feed on aphids. Males hover persistently at head height.

fm.Color: Black with narrow yellow side marks

hp: Hover fly — adult pollinates flowers by hovering. Larvae eat aphids (biocontrol) or decompose organic matter.

desc: Bee-mimic hover fly with boldly striped thorax. Rat-tailed maggot larvae in shallow water and wet organic matter. Found near streams and ponds.

fm.Color: Bold yellow-black thoracic stripes; dark face stripe
fm.Habitat: Wet areas — streams, ponds, wet gardens

hp: Stream and river habitats. Aquatic or semi-aquatic lifestyle. Bee mimic.

desc: Medium hover fly with smoke-tinted wings. Important aphid predator in mountain meadows. Common in foothill and mountain areas.

fm.Color: Dark with yellow lunules; smoky wings

hp: Aphid predator — important biocontrol agent. Preys on aphid. Montane.

desc: Large hover fly mimicking bald-faced hornets. Visits flowers. Harmless. Yellow-and-black pattern with wasp-like antenna waving behavior.

fm.Color: Black+yellow wasp pattern; waves antennae like wasp

hp: Ambushes prey on flowers. Ant mimic — may prey on ants. Wasp mimic.

desc: Small dark hover fly. Larvae bore into onion, garlic, and flower bulbs. Less conspicuous than Narcissus fly but equally destructive.
hp: Flower pollinator and hover fly mimic. Larvae variable.

desc: Tiny hover fly with distinctive calligraphy-like abdominal markings. Very common in gardens. Larvae eat aphids and thrips.

fm.Color: Yellow with intricate black abdominal calligraphy

hp: Aphid predator — important biological control agent in crops and gardens. Preys on aphid. Tiny.

desc: Yellow-faced hover fly — slender, elegant wasp-mimic syrphid with bright yellow face and unusually long antennae for a hover fly. The antennae enhance the wasp-like appearance. Visits flowers. Larvae feed on root aphids. Less common than other Chrysotoxum.

fm.Color: Black and yellow wasp-mimic; long antennae

hp: Ant mimic — Batesian mimicry in body shape and gait.

desc: Elongated hover fly — male abdomen extends well past wing tips. Yellow with black bands. Very common at flowers. Fast agile flier.
hp: Builds horizontal sheet/dome web. Ambushes prey on flowers. Elongated.

desc: Spectacular bumblebee mimic — dense orange-brown thoracic pile. Very convincing. Visits flowers in spring. Larvae in tree holes.
hp: Ambushes prey on flowers. Bee mimic. Spectacular.

desc: Small hover fly with comma-shaped yellow marks on dark abdomen. Very common in agricultural areas. Larvae are voracious aphid predators.

fm.Color: Dark with yellow comma marks on abdomen

hp: Aphid predator — important biocontrol agent. Preys on aphid. Family Syrphinae.
